Q1: I usually tried to set up a pretty consistent study routine, dedicating about three hours each weekday to coursework. I'm a visual learner, so I'd organize my notes with color-coding and diagrams, which took some extra time but helped me remember things better. I'd typically review lecture materials first, then work on assignments. Honestly, even though I put in the hours, sometimes I felt like I was just going through the motions rather than being truly excited about it.
Q2: My pattern was mostly regular, but I'd definitely shift things around when deadlines were coming up. I tried to keep weekday evenings for studying, but sometimes my extracurricular activities would push things to weekends. When multiple assignments piled up, I'd have to extend my study sessions, which sometimes felt exhausting. I preferred routine, but life didn't always cooperate with that plan.
Q3: Usually I'd log in to check if there were new announcements or to access the lecture videos and slides. Sometimes I'd get an email notification about a deadline or discussion post, and that would prompt me to go check. I also logged in regularly just to see if any grades had been posted. It became kind of automatic to check in most days, even if I didn't always do a lot once I was there.
Q4: Yeah, after the first few weeks, logging into the LMS became pretty automatic for me. I'd usually check it in the morning with my coffee, just to see what was happening. The discussion posts helped make it feel more social, so I'd want to see if anyone had replied to my comments. It just became part of my daily routine, like checking messages or email.
Q5: The video lectures were definitely the most useful for me since I learn best by seeing things visually. I'd watch them and pause to take notes or sketch out diagrams. The slides were also really helpful because I could print them and annotate them by hand. Discussion boards were nice too because sometimes other students would explain things in ways that clicked better for me.
Q6: I honestly didn't use the optional readings very much. They felt like extra work when I was already spending a lot of time on the core materials. Some of the supplementary links to external websites also got ignored because I wasn't sure how relevant they'd be. If something wasn't clearly connected to assignments or exams, I'd usually skip it to save time.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see how it connected to what I needed to learn for exams or assignments. Visual materials like diagrams or video explanations were always more appealing to me. I also valued activities that let me interact with other students because it made the learning feel less isolating. If something felt like busywork with no clear purpose, I'd lose interest quickly.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different because there was no pressure attached to them. I'd sometimes look at them out of curiosity, but without a deadline, they'd usually slip to the bottom of my priority list. Required activities had more urgency, so I'd make sure to complete them even if I wasn't feeling motivated. The optional stuff felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford time-wise.
Q9: Definitely yes. I'd download the slides and videos so I could study offline, especially when I wanted to review on my tablet without needing internet. I also made a lot of handwritten notes and flashcards that I'd study from. Sometimes I'd use YouTube videos to understand concepts better when the course materials weren't clicking for me. A lot of my studying happened away from the LMS.
Q10: There were many times I'd be reviewing my printed notes or going through flashcards I'd made, and none of that would show up anywhere. I'd also sometimes study with a classmate in person, quizzing each other on material. When I was drawing diagrams to understand processes, that was real learning but completely invisible online. The LMS only captured a fraction of what I was actually doing.
Q11: Yes, I did this a lot actually. I'd watch a video once online, then download it or take detailed notes and never go back to the LMS version. Same with slides—I'd download them once and then study from the printed copies for weeks. It was just more convenient to have everything accessible offline, especially when I was studying in places without good internet.
Q12: There were definitely moments when engagement got harder, usually when I had a lot going on with my extracurricular activities. Sometimes I just felt unmotivated even though my stress wasn't super high—it's hard to explain, but I'd feel kind of flat about the coursework. The CBT session I had actually helped me recognize some of these patterns and work through them a bit. When I was feeling low, even simple tasks felt heavy.
Q13: Fatigue was probably the biggest factor for me. After a long day with classes and activities, I'd log into the LMS but not really absorb anything. When assignments piled up, I'd focus on what was due soonest and ignore other materials. Confusion about certain topics also made me avoid those sections until I absolutely had to face them. It wasn't that I didn't care—I was just stretched thin sometimes.
Q14: Oh yeah, there were times I'd be watching a video but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd technically be logged in and the video would be playing, but I wasn't actually learning anything. Sometimes I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them carefully. Those moments of passive engagement probably looked good in the data but weren't meaningful at all.
Q15: I definitely postponed working on assignments that felt overwhelming or confusing. If I didn't understand a topic well, I'd put off the related work because facing it made me anxious. Sometimes I'd avoid discussion posts because I didn't feel confident enough in my understanding to contribute. I knew these things were important, but my low motivation made it easy to justify waiting until later.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the main driver of my LMS activity. I'd definitely be more active in the days leading up to a due date, watching videos and reviewing materials more intensively. Without a deadline, I'd spread things out more casually and sometimes let tasks slip. The structure of having deadlines helped me stay on track even when my motivation was low.
Q17: Feedback did affect me, though sometimes it took me a while to act on it. When I got good feedback, it motivated me to keep going with similar effort. Constructive criticism sometimes made me feel a bit down initially, but eventually I'd use it to improve. I'd usually check my grades pretty quickly after they were posted because I wanted to know where I stood.
Q18: The weekly structure of the course helped me stay organized because I knew what to expect each week. Having clear modules with videos and assignments made it easier to plan my time. The discussion requirements pushed me to engage with classmates, which I might not have done otherwise. Overall, the structure provided a framework that helped compensate for my sometimes low motivation.
Q19: Having visual materials like videos and diagrams made engagement much easier for me given how I learn. Clear instructions on assignments also helped a lot. What made things harder was when readings were really text-heavy without visual aids—I'd struggle to stay focused. Also, when the connection between different modules wasn't clear, I'd feel a bit lost about how everything fit together.
Q20: I think some students might be trying to figure things out but struggling to actually complete the work. They could be watching videos and reading materials but not understanding enough to submit assignments confidently. Or maybe they're dealing with personal stuff that makes it hard to follow through. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity—sometimes it's just trying to make sense of confusing content.
Q21: That actually kind of reflects my experience in some ways. I did a lot of studying offline with downloaded materials and handwritten notes, so my LMS activity might not show everything. Some students are just efficient—they know what they need and get it done without spending extra time browsing. I think you can learn effectively without constantly being logged in if you have good study habits.
Q22: I think course logs can show patterns like when students are most active and which resources get used most. They can track completion of tasks and maybe identify students who are falling behind. But they miss all the offline studying, the quality of engagement, and the emotional state of students. Someone could be logged in but completely checked out mentally, and the data wouldn't know the difference.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks to get them done. It's when I'm genuinely curious about a topic and want to learn more, not just pass. For me, engagement also involves connecting with classmates and feeling like part of a learning community. It's a mental and emotional investment, not just physical presence or clicking through materials.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much time I spent studying from printed notes and handwritten flashcards. It also wouldn't capture my emotional state—like how sometimes I felt happy but unmotivated, or how the CBT session helped me process some struggles. My conversations with classmates outside the platform, the diagrams I drew to understand concepts, and my genuine interest in certain topics versus just going through the motions—none of that shows up in the data.
